Technical notes (work in progress)
Earlier technical notes are currently hosted on RPubs. Over time, strongest pieces will be migrated into this site as Quarto articles for better search visibility and consistent presentation.

title: Nathan Machuki Onduma
text: 15+ years | Multi-country programs | Statistics & Data Science | MEAL | Organizational Performance

title: Nathan Machuki Onduma
text:
Leadership and areas of focus


Selected Leadership Highlights

Led evidence and analytics initiatives supporting programs reaching 150M+ people annually.
Directed multi-country measurement, evaluation, learning, and performance functions across Africa.
Supported organizational strategy through evidence generation, forecasting, and decision-support analytics.
Strengthened data governance, quality assurance, and accountability systems across complex portfolios.
Built capacity for data use, learning, and adaptive management among technical and program teams.



Current Areas of Focus

Impact Measurement & Organizational Performance
Evidence Generation & Learning Systems
Adaptive Management
Data Governance
Strategic Analytics
AI for Development and Public Health
Decision Intelligence for Social Impact





MEL leadership
Multi-country evidence, learning, and performance systems supporting adaptive management, partner performance oversight, and strategic decision-making across health and development programs.


Data science
Quantitative and qualitative analysis and applied data science supporting evidence generation, implementation learning, and strategic decision-making across development programs using statistical modelling, survey analytics, R, and Power BI.


Systems Strengthening
Global health systems strengthening through evidence use, digital transformation, and implementation data systems across NTD, HIV, and RMNCAH programs using DHIS2, EMR systems, KoboToolbox, and ODK.

title: Nathan Machuki Onduma
text:
Featured work


Neglected tropical diseases and MEL systems
This work focuses on MEL strategy, evidence generation, implementation learning, and performance analytics for neglected tropical disease programs, including intestinal worms, schistosomiasis, lympatic filariasis, river blindness, trachoma and visceral leishmaniasis.
Read the CV


Projects, Case Studies and Aanlytics
A portfolio of selected digital transformation examples and analytical works.More will be uploaded to github on a rolling basis.
View projects

title: Nathan Machuki Onduma
text:
Senior Impact, Evidence & Learning Leader | MEAL | Organizational Performance | Statistics & Data Science
Kisii, Kenya | +254 728 352 614 | nonduma@yahoo.com
LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/nathan-onduma-7a80a0107

Download PDF Download Word




Senior impact, evidence, learning and analytics leader with 15+ years of experience strengthening organizational performance, evaluation systems, and evidence-informed decision-making across Africa. Experienced leading multi-country MEL and analytics functions, developing measurement frameworks, supporting Theory of Change processes, generating strategic insights for senior leadership, board and donors, and building data systems that improve program effectiveness at scale. Combines strong statistical and data science expertise with practical experience translating evidence into action.





Led MEL, evidence generation and performance measurement initiatives across programs reaching more than 150 million people annually.
Supported organizational Theory of Change development and results framework design.
Generated evidence and analytics informing strategic planning, investment cases, localization initiatives and organizational decision-making.
Strengthened data governance, reporting architectures and accountability systems across multi-country portfolios.
Produced evidence products for donors, executive leadership and board-level reporting.
Built capacity for data use, learning and adaptive management across diverse teams and partners.





Impact Measurement & Performance Management
Monitoring, Evaluation, Accountability and Learning (MEAL)
Organizational Learning & Adaptive Management
Theory of Change & Results Frameworks
Evaluation & Research Methods
Evidence Generation & Decision Support
Data Governance & Quality Assurance
Applied Statistics & Data Science
Stakeholder Engagement & Capacity Strengthening
Multi-Country Program Leadership
Strategic Donor Reporting & Accountability






Senior Associate Director, Measurement, Evaluation, and Learning
March 2025 – Present | Remote, Kenya

Design and lead MEL strategy and implementation for six NTD programs within multi‑country initiatives spanning 30+ countries, supporting over $50M in disbursements and reaching 150M+ individuals annually.
Oversee development of MEL frameworks, indicators, and data systems to strengthen program performance and impact measurement.
Lead the design and strengthening of country-owned measurement and health information systems across 30+ countries, establishing standardized indicators, reporting architectures, and data governance approaches that improve decision-making, accountability, and long-term sustainability.
Generate strategic insights and evidence products that inform organizational planning, donor engagement, executive decision-making, and board oversight.
Provide strategic technical assistance to Ministries of Health to strengthen routine health information systems, improve DHIS2 data quality and utilization, and institutionalize evidence-informed decision-making processes.
Provide data‑driven technical support for fund development, including guidance during the proposal development stage.
Co-lead the development and refinement of the organization’s Theory of Change, strengthening organizational learning, adaptive management, and evidence-informed decision-making.
Facilitate cross-country learning and synthesize implementation evidence to generate actionable insights that strengthen adaptive management and inform strategic decision-making.




Associate Director, Measurement, Evaluation, and Learning
October 2022 – February 2025 | Remote, Kenya

Designed and implemented MEL systems across 30+ low‑resource countries with limited M&E capacity, strengthening data use for programmatic decision‑making.
Led digitization initiatives across Nigeria and DRC, enhancing data collection and reporting for five PC NTD programs.
Supported integration of visceral leishmaniasis indicators into national HMIS platforms.
Contributed to major investment cases and strategic documents that informed fundraising and program prioritization.
Strengthened partner accountability via performance tracking and data reviews, aligning country MEL systems with global MEL frameworks and donor reporting standards.
Developed and managed quick‑use data tools, including Google Sheets and Excel dashboards, to support program teams.

Selected Achievements

Advanced multi-country data systems, including indicator frameworks, data tools, shapefiles, and training resources.
Led analytical work that informed organizational localization strategy and strengthened evidence-based decision-making across departments.
Supported development and refinement of the organizational Theory of Change, ensuring alignment between strategic priorities, performance measurement, and evidence use.
Led segments of the DHIS2-based program database development in collaboration with BAO systems, the technical contractor.
Facilitated cross-country learning, synthesized implementation evidence, and generated programmatic insights that strengthened adaptive management and informed strategic policy, program, and investment decisions across multiple countries.
Produced evidence briefs, learning products, technical reports and presentations used by donors, ministries of health and program leadership to guide strategic decisions.
Promoted organizational learning and adaptive management through synthesis of monitoring, evaluation, and analytical findings into actionable recommendations.




Statistician / MEL Specialist
October 2020 – October 2022

Delivered statistical and MEL consulting services for global health and development programs funded by USAID, World Bank, and other donors and partners.
Led the design and implementation of baseline, midline, endline and special studies using mixed-methods approaches, including quarterly quantitative surveys, routine program data analysis, qualitative assessments and implementation research.
Translated complex analytical outputs into clear, actionable insights for technical and non-technical stakeholders.
Provided advisory support on monitoring, measurement, evaluation, research, and learning for public, private, and non-profit organizations.
Provided technical leadership on planning and conducting a Harmonized Health Facility Assessment (HHFA) and third party monitoring




Strategic Information Advisor
February 2017 – October 2020 | Kenya, PACT Timiza Program

Led MEL strategy and implementation for a USG PEPFAR/CDC-funded HIV program.
Directed data systems including DATIM, DHIS2, and EMR platforms to ensure accurate and timely reporting.
Managed a multidisciplinary team of MEL and data professionals.
Delivered technical assistance to county health systems, enhancing data collection, analysis, and reporting of key performance indicators (KPIs).
Led baseline assessments, data quality assessments, routine reporting, and stakeholder review meetings.

Selected Achievements

Increased EMR system coverage from below 20% to over 70%.
Owned the development and deployment of the DHIS2-based Data Reporting and Warehousing System (DRAWS) with BAO systems, the technical contractor.
Part of the technical working group for the development and deployment of CDC Kenya’s Partner Platform for Performance Monitoring (3PM).
Led the seamless interoperability of multiple information management systems—including KenyaEMR, Laboratory Information Management System (LIMS), Logistics Management Information System (LMIS), KHIS, and the National Data Warehouse—to ensure high-quality data across platforms for informed decision-making.
Designed and implemented a comprehensive Evaluation and Performance Monitoring Plan.
Secured continued donor funding through high-quality reporting and performance tracking.
Supervised 2 data managers, 7 M&E officers, and 1 HMIS specialist.




Monitoring and Evaluation Officer
April 2016 – January 2017 | Kisumu, Kenya

Developed and managed monitoring and evaluation systems for donor-funded Reproductive, Maternal, Newborn, Child, and Adolescent Health (RMNCAH) programs.
Developed indicators, data collection tools, reporting matrices, and institutional data systems.
Led implementation and evaluation of KMET programs empowering adolescent girls and young women (AGYW) aged 10–24 years across Kenya, supporting the establishment of youth-friendly clinics in collaboration with county Departments of Health.
Led training and capacity building for program staff, service delivery staff, and partners.
Supported proposal development, grant reporting, data quality audits, and research dissemination.

Selected Achievements

Improved DHIS2 reporting quality and coverage from 55% to 94% within five months.
Recognized as top-performing partner in reporting quality and timeliness across 8 implementing partners.
Supervised 2 M&E assistants directly and 30 data clerks indirectly.




Economist/Statistician
September 2010 – April 2016 | Kenya

Led county and district-level statistical analysis, planning, monitoring, and reporting functions.
Developed program monitoring tools, performance indicators, and policy inputs.
Coordinated development, review, monitoring, and evaluation of District Development Plans and County Integrated Development Plans across key government thematic areas including education, gender mainstreaming among others.
Supported budgeting, strategic planning, feasibility studies, and sector statistics dissemination.

Selected Achievements

Led the production of Nyamira County’s inaugural Statistical Abstract in collaboration with the Kenya National Bureau of Statistics providing a foundation for evidence-based economic planning.
Led the development of the inaugural County Integrated Development Plan, multi-year (2013/2014 to 2015/2016) budget policy documents, and strategic frameworks, plans and reports, ensuring alignment with county and national priorities.
Developed Nyamira County M&E indicators in partnership with UNDP and UN-Women to support tracking of the key performance indicators.
Founded the Borabu District Information and Documentation Centre, improving transparency and accessibility of government development plans and strategies for civil servants and citizens.







Master of Science in Statistics, Kenyatta University, Kenya — 2014
Bachelor of Science in Statistics, Second Class Upper, University of Nairobi, Kenya — 2009
Kenya Certificate of Secondary Education, A Plain, Nyamagwa Boys’ High School, Kenya — 2003






Programming & Analysis: R tidyverse ecosystem, Excel/Google Sheets, Power BI
Health information systems (HMIS/DHIS2, EMR, LMIS, eCHIS) and digital data collection tools (KoboToolbox, ODK)
Enterprise Systems: Salesforce
Research & Reporting: Survey design, statistical modelling, data visualization, Quarto






Time Series Forecasting — Prof. Bahman Rostami-Tabar, Nairobi, Kenya, 2023
Demographic and Health Surveys (DHS) Program online courses, 2022
Leadership and Management in Health — University of Washington, 2020
Introduction to Epidemiology for Global Health — University of Washington, 2018
Monitoring and Evaluation fundamentals, frameworks, data management, and analysis — CDC Kenya, Ministry of Health, Global Health Learning Center, and related partners
Spatial Statistics — Prof. Thomas Achia, CDC Kenya, 2017






Duncan Ochol, Kebron Haile, Nathan Onduma, Tsegaye Yohanes, & Kimberly Kamara (2025). Elimination of visceral leishmaniasis in Ethiopia: cross-sector collaboration and cost sharing to promote sustainability. PubMed
Makau-Barasa, L. K., Onduma, N., Yotebieng, K., & Karutu, C. (2023). African institutions will lead on the road to end neglected tropical diseases. Frontiers
Onduma, N. M. (2019–2022). RPubs publications and technical notes on statistical and data science methodologies. RPubs.
Onduma, N. M. (2014). Mixture regression-cum-ratio estimators using multi-auxiliary variables and attributes in two-phase sampling. MSc project, Kenyatta University.
Mshambala, A. W., Onduma, N. M., & Ng’ang’a, J. G. (2009). Online presence of internet consumers in Nairobi’s Central Business District. Undergraduate project, University of Nairobi.






English — Full professional proficiency
Kiswahili — Full professional proficiency
French — Basic proficiency






2019: Recognized by the University of Maryland Kenya Country Director as “First Among Equals” for leadership of the Strategic Information Department.
2007: Awarded university fees waiver after being recognized as the Best Second-Year Student, School of Mathematics, University of Nairobi.
2006: Best First-Year Student, Statistics Section, University of Nairobi.
2003: Ranked second-best KCSE student in Gucha District, Kenya.





Available upon request.

title: Nathan Machuki Onduma

title: Projects, Case Studies & Analytics Portfolio
text:
Selected examples of how evidence, analytics, and digital systems have been used to strengthen program implementation, organizational learning, and decision-making across global health and development programs. - Nigeria Digital Monitoring Systems - Localization analytics for Organizational Learning - DRC Context - Kenya HIV digitization - - (future) Forecasting & decision support





Mass Drug Administration (MDA) and Morbidity Management and Disability Prevention (MMDP) programs relied heavily on paper-based reporting and donor-supported digital tools. While an initial pilot using CommCare demonstrated the value of digitization, concerns remained around long-term sustainability, government ownership, integration with national systems, and ongoing operational costs.



Working closely with the Ministry of Health, program partners and colleagues, I supported the transition from a standalone CommCare implementation to a DHIS2-based platform aligned with the national health information system architecture.
The initiative focused on strengthening government ownership, reducing dependence on parallel donor-supported systems, integrating community-level monitoring into existing national reporting structures, and enabling near real-time visibility of implementation performance.




Strengthened government ownership and sustainability of digital monitoring systems.
Improved reporting timeliness and data availability during implementation.
Enhanced data quality through standardized reporting and validation processes.
Reduced reliance on parallel systems and external infrastructure.
Increased visibility of community-level program performance through near real-time dashboards.




Supported the transition of community-based NTD monitoring toward a more sustainable and scalable monitoring architecture aligned with existing national health information systems.
Tools: DHIS2 | R | Data Quality Audits | Dashboarding






As development organizations increasingly invest in localization, leadership requires reliable evidence to understand how funding patterns, implementation performance, and institutional capacity evolve over time. However, programmatic and financial data are often dispersed across multiple systems, making it difficult to monitor progress and inform strategic decisions.



I designed and operationalized a routine analytics workflow that integrated program implementation and financial data to support organizational learning and localization monitoring.
The framework integrated financial, operational, and programmatic data from multiple countries to support organizational learning and strategic decision-making.
To improve accessibility and use, analytical outputs were automated and shared through user-friendly reporting products that supported both technical teams and senior decision-makers.




Trends in program implementation and resource utilization.
Relationships between operational investments and program outcomes.
Geographic variation in implementation performance.
Evidence supporting organizational learning and sustainability discussions.
Routine performance monitoring to support strategic planning.





Strengthened routine use of data for strategic planning and organizational learning.
Improved visibility of localization-related performance trends.
Enabled evidence-informed discussions among leadership teams.
Reduced manual reporting effort through reproducible analytical workflows.
Increased access to timely performance information across departments.




Supported organizational leadership in monitoring localization progress, assessing implementation trends, and strengthening evidence-informed decision-making.
Tools: R | Tidyverse | Quarto | Salesforce | Google Sheets | Statistical Analysis | Data Visualization






NTD programs in the Democratic Republic of Congo operate across highly remote and logistically complex environments where connectivity constraints, infrastructure limitations, and diverse implementation contexts create significant challenges for digital monitoring and reporting.



Working with government stakeholders, colleagues and a technical partner, I supported a comprehensive digital landscape assessment to understand monitoring workflows, technology readiness, reporting bottlenecks, and operational realities across multiple implementation settings.
The assessment informed the design of a tailored digital transformation strategy that balanced standardization with the flexibility required in different operating environments.



The assessment identified substantial variation in digital readiness, reporting practices, infrastructure capacity, and monitoring requirements across implementation areas. Findings highlighted the need for context-driven digital solutions rather than a one-size-fits-all technology approach.




Identified critical gaps in monitoring architecture and reporting systems.
Strengthened understanding of infrastructure and capacity requirements.
Informed the design of a tailored digital monitoring strategy.
Improved alignment between program needs and digital solutions.
Supported future investment decisions in digital health and monitoring systems.




Enabled stakeholders to move from technology-led digitization toward evidence-based digital transformation grounded in implementation realities.
Tools: Digital Landscape Assessment | DHIS2 | Stakeholder Analysis | Systems Design




During my work supporting a USG PEPFAR/CDC-funded HIV program in Kenya, I critically assessed evidence surrounding the digitization of HIV service delivery and reporting systems intended to improve data quality, program monitoring, and decision-making. The initiative transitioned a large network of health facilities across rural Kenya from fragmented paper-based systems to a DHIS2-based platform and centralized data reporting and warehousing system.
Initial evidence suggested digitization would improve reporting accuracy, timeliness, and overall program performance. However, I identified important limitations in the evidence base. Much of the literature demonstrated improvements in reporting completeness and data availability, but there was limited rigorous evidence directly linking digitization to sustained patient outcomes such as retention in care or viral suppression. In addition, many successful digital health implementations described in the literature were conducted in settings with stronger infrastructure and technical capacity than the rural facilities where we operated.
During implementation, we encountered several challenges that directly affected the feasibility of scaling the system, including inconsistent historical paper records, low digital literacy among some health workers, unreliable connectivity, and weak facility-level data governance. Several remote facilities lacked reliable electricity, requiring investment in solar systems to support paperless operations. As a result, we adopted a phased implementation approach that classified facilities according to workload and infrastructure readiness, strengthened data quality audits, and introduced context-appropriate innovations such as SMS-based reporting into DHIS2 platforms.
The experience showed me that digital transformation alone is insufficient for health systems improvement; sustainable impact depends equally on infrastructure, governance, workforce capacity, and implementation approaches that fit local realities.

Enabled evidence-informed discussions among leadership teams.
Reduced manual reporting effort through reproducible analytical workflows.
Increased access to timely performance information across departments.




Supported organizational leadership in monitoring localization progress, assessing implementation trends, and strengthening evidence-informed decision-making.
Tools: R | Tidyverse | Quarto | Salesforce | Google Sheets | Statistical Analysis | Data Visualization






NTD programs in the Democratic Republic of Congo operate across highly remote and logistically complex environments where connectivity constraints, infrastructure limitations, and diverse implementation contexts create significant challenges for digital monitoring and reporting.



Working with government stakeholders, colleagues and a technical partner, I supported a comprehensive digital landscape assessment to understand monitoring workflows, technology readiness, reporting bottlenecks, and operational realities across multiple implementation settings.
The assessment informed the design of a tailored digital transformation strategy that balanced standardization with the flexibility required in different operating environments.



The assessment identified substantial variation in digital readiness, reporting practices, infrastructure capacity, and monitoring requirements across implementation areas. Findings highlighted the need for context-driven digital solutions rather than a one-size-fits-all technology approach.




Identified critical gaps in monitoring architecture and reporting systems.
Strengthened understanding of infrastructure and capacity requirements.
Informed the design of a tailored digital monitoring strategy.
Improved alignment between program needs and digital solutions.
Supported future investment decisions in digital health and monitoring systems.




Enabled stakeholders to move from technology-led digitization toward evidence-based digital transformation grounded in implementation realities.
Tools: Digital Landscape Assessment | DHIS2 | Stakeholder Analysis | Systems Design




During my work supporting a USG PEPFAR/CDC-funded HIV program in Kenya, I critically assessed evidence surrounding the digitization of HIV service delivery and reporting systems intended to improve data quality, program monitoring, and decision-making. The initiative transitioned a large network of health facilities across rural Kenya from fragmented paper-based systems to a DHIS2-based platform and centralized data reporting and warehousing system.
Initial evidence suggested digitization would improve reporting accuracy, timeliness, and overall program performance. However, I identified important limitations in the evidence base. Much of the literature demonstrated improvements in reporting completeness and data availability, but there was limited rigorous evidence directly linking digitization to sustained patient outcomes such as retention in care or viral suppression. In addition, many successful digital health implementations described in the literature were conducted in settings with stronger infrastructure and technical capacity than the rural facilities where we operated.
During implementation, we encountered several challenges that directly affected the feasibility of scaling the system, including inconsistent historical paper records, low digital literacy among some health workers, unreliable connectivity, and weak facility-level data governance. Several remote facilities lacked reliable electricity, requiring investment in solar systems to support paperless operations. As a result, we adopted a phased implementation approach that classified facilities according to workload and infrastructure readiness, strengthened data quality audits, and introduced context-appropriate innovations such as SMS-based reporting into DHIS2 platforms.
The experience showed me that digital transformation alone is insufficient for health systems improvement; sustainable impact depends equally on infrastructure, governance, workforce capacity, and implementation approaches that fit local realities.
